title: LiveData & RxJava Deep Comparison
description: RxJava types and patterns, LiveData lifecycle magic, full comparison, and migration to Flow/StateFlow

references.title: Android Developers — Migrate from LiveData to Flow
Android Developers — Kotlin Flows on Android
Jake Wharton — Why I stopped using RxJava

lesson:
RxJava in Depth — The Five Observable Types

RxJava provides five specialized types for different emission patterns. Understanding which one to use is half the battle:

TypeEmitsUse WhenExample
Observable<T>0..N items, then completes or errorsMultiple results over timeSearch suggestions, WebSocket messages
Single<T>Exactly 1 item or errorOne-shot API callapi.getUser(id)
Maybe<T>0 or 1 item, then completesCache lookup (might miss)cache.findUser(id)
CompletableNo items — just completes or errorsSide effect with no resultdb.deleteUser(id)
Flowable<T>0..N items with backpressureHigh-throughput streams where consumer is slower than producerSensor data, file streaming
Observable vs Flowable: Use Observable for UI events and low-volume streams. Use Flowable only when the producer can outpace the consumer (e.g., sensor data at 100Hz). Flowable adds overhead for backpressure strategies (BUFFER, DROP, LATEST, ERROR).

The RxJava Pattern: The Four-Stage Pipeline

Every RxJava chain follows the same pattern. Think of it as a factory assembly line:

subscribeOn(Schedulers.io()) ← Which thread PRODUCES data │ operators ← Transform data (map, filter, flatMap...) │ observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) ← Which thread RECEIVES data │ subscribe { result → } ← Consume the final result
// Classic RxJava API call pattern api.searchUsers(query) // Returns Observable<List<User>> .subscribeOn(Schedulers.io()) // Run network call on IO thread .map { users -> users.filter { it.active } } // Transform .debounce(300, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS) // Wait for typing pause .distinctUntilChanged() // Skip duplicate queries .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) // Switch to main for UI .subscribe( { users -> showResults(users) }, // onNext { error -> showError(error) } // onError )
subscribeOn vs observeOn confusion: subscribeOn affects the source (upstream) — where data is produced. It only matters once, no matter where you place it. observeOn affects everything downstream — where data is consumed. Multiple observeOn calls switch threads at different points in the chain.

RxJava Error Handling

// Error recovery operators api.getUser(id) .onErrorReturn { defaultUser } // Replace error with default .onErrorResumeNext { fallbackApi.get() } // Switch to backup source .retry(3) // Retry up to 3 times .retryWhen { errors -> errors.zipWith(Observable.range(1, 3)) { _, i -> i } .flatMap { Observable.timer(it.toLong(), TimeUnit.SECONDS) } } // Exponential backoff (verbose!) .subscribe({ ... }, { ... })

Why RxJava Fell Out of Favor

RxJava was revolutionary (2015-2019), but it came with serious costs that ultimately drove the ecosystem toward coroutines:

The Five Pain Points:
1. Memory leaks via CompositeDisposable: Every subscription returns a Disposable you MUST manually manage. Forget to dispose in onDestroy()? Memory leak. This was the #1 source of bugs.

2. Brutal learning curve: RxJava has 400+ operators. flatMap vs switchMap vs concatMap vs exhaustMap? mergeWith vs ambWith vs zipWith? New developers were paralyzed by choice.

3. Binary size: RxJava 2 adds ~2.4 MB to your APK. RxAndroid adds more. For many apps, this was significant.

4. Unreadable stack traces: An exception inside a flatMap produces a 30+ line stack trace through internal RxJava machinery. Finding your actual code is like finding a needle in a haystack.

5. subscribeOn/observeOn confusion: Placing them wrong produces subtle threading bugs that pass all tests but crash in production.
// The CompositeDisposable boilerplate every Activity/Fragment needed class UserActivity : AppCompatActivity() { private val disposables = CompositeDisposable() override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { disposables.add( api.getUser(id) .subscribeOn(Schedulers.io()) .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) .subscribe({ updateUi(it) }, { showError(it) }) ) } override fun onDestroy() { super.onDestroy() disposables.clear() // FORGET THIS → MEMORY LEAK } }

LiveData in Depth

LiveData is a lifecycle-aware observable data holder. It was Google's answer to RxJava's lifecycle problems:

// ViewModel exposes LiveData class UserViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _user = MutableLiveData<User>() // Private mutable val user: LiveData<User> = _user // Public read-only fun loadUser(id: String) { viewModelScope.launch { val result = repository.getUser(id) _user.value = result // setValue: MUST be on main thread _user.postValue(result) // postValue: works from ANY thread } } } // Fragment observes with lifecycle owner class UserFragment : Fragment() { override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { viewModel.user.observe(viewLifecycleOwner) { user -> // Only called when Fragment is STARTED or RESUMED binding.nameText.text = user.name } } }
setValue vs postValue:
setValue() — synchronous, must be called from main thread. If you call it twice, observers see BOTH values.
postValue() — posts to main thread handler. If you call it twice quickly, observers may only see the LAST value (the first is overwritten before dispatch).

LiveData Transformations

LiveData has a limited but useful set of transformation operators:

// map — transform the value (synchronous) val userName: LiveData<String> = user.map { it.name } // switchMap — transform to another LiveData (async) val userPosts: LiveData<List<Post>> = userId.switchMap { id -> repository.getPostsLiveData(id) // Returns LiveData<List<Post>> } // MediatorLiveData — combine multiple sources (verbose!) val profileState = MediatorLiveData<ProfileState>() init { profileState.addSource(user) { u -> profileState.value = ProfileState( user = u, posts = posts.value ?: emptyList() ) } profileState.addSource(posts) { p -> profileState.value = ProfileState( user = user.value ?: return@addSource, posts = p ) } }
MediatorLiveData is painfully verbose. Combining two LiveData sources is ~15 lines. Combining three or four becomes unmanageable. This was one of the strongest arguments for migrating to Flow, where combine(flow1, flow2) { a, b -> } does the same thing in one line.

LiveData's Lifecycle Magic

The killer feature of LiveData is automatic lifecycle awareness:

Fragment State: CREATED → STARTED → RESUMED → STARTED → CREATED → DESTROYED LiveData dispatches: ✗ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✗ ✗ ↑ ↑ Starts observing Auto-unsubscribes ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Key behaviors: 1. Only dispatches in STARTED or RESUMED state → No crashes from updating a dead Fragment's views 2. Replays the LATEST value to new observers → Screen rotation: new Fragment immediately gets current data 3. Auto-removes observer at DESTROYED → No memory leaks, no CompositeDisposable needed
viewLifecycleOwner vs this: In Fragments, ALWAYS use viewLifecycleOwner, not this. The Fragment can outlive its View (e.g., on the back stack). Using this means the old observer is never removed, and you get duplicate observers after each navigation return.

Full Comparison: LiveData vs RxJava vs Flow

FeatureRxJavaLiveDataFlow / StateFlow
Lifecycle-awareNo — manual disposeYes — built-inYes — via repeatOnLifecycle
ThreadingsubscribeOn / observeOnMain thread only (postValue for bg)flowOn (upstream only)
Operators400+ operatorsmap, switchMap, MediatorLiveData~50 operators (map, filter, combine, flatMapLatest...)
BackpressureFlowable with strategiesNo (always latest value)Built into Flow (suspends producer)
TestingTestObserver, TestSchedulerInstantTaskExecutorRuleTurbine library, runTest
Null safetyNo (RxJava 2+ bans null)Yes — allows null valuesYes — nullable generics
Compose supportsubscribeAsState() (adapter)observeAsState()collectAsStateWithLifecycle() (best)
Error handlingonError callback, operatorsNo built-in error channelcatch { }, onCompletion { }
Cold/HotBoth (Observable=cold, Subject=hot)Hot onlyBoth (Flow=cold, StateFlow=hot)
Binary size~2.4 MB~100 KB (part of lifecycle)~200 KB (part of coroutines)
Stack traces30+ lines, unreadableSimpleSimple (sequential code)

RxJava → Flow Migration Cheat Sheet

If your codebase uses RxJava, here's how each concept maps to Kotlin Flow:

RxJavaKotlin Flow Equivalent
Observable.create { emitter -> }flow { emit(value) }
Single.just(value)flowOf(value) or suspend fun
subscribeOn(Schedulers.io())flowOn(Dispatchers.IO)
observeOn(mainThread())Not needed — collect on Main via scope
flatMap { }flatMapConcat { }
switchMap { }flatMapLatest { }
CompositeDisposableStructured concurrency (scope cancellation)
BehaviorSubjectMutableStateFlow
PublishSubjectMutableSharedFlow
ReplaySubject(1)MutableSharedFlow(replay = 1)
Flowable (backpressure)Flow with buffer() / conflate()
.dispose()Cancel the coroutine scope
TestObserverTurbine: flow.test { }
// RxJava (before) fun searchUsers(query: String): Observable<List<User>> = Observable.create { emitter -> val result = api.search(query) emitter.onNext(result) emitter.onComplete() } // Kotlin Flow (after) fun searchUsers(query: String): Flow<List<User>> = flow { val result = api.search(query) emit(result) }

LiveData → StateFlow Migration Pattern

Google now recommends StateFlow over LiveData for new code. Here's the migration pattern:

// ═══════ BEFORE: LiveData ═══════ class UserViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _uiState = MutableLiveData(UiState.Loading) val uiState: LiveData<UiState> = _uiState fun loadUser(id: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = UiState.Loading try { val user = repository.getUser(id) _uiState.value = UiState.Success(user) } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = UiState.Error(e.message) } } } } // Fragment (LiveData) viewModel.uiState.observe(viewLifecycleOwner) { state -> when (state) { is UiState.Loading -> showLoading() is UiState.Success -> showUser(state.user) is UiState.Error -> showError(state.message) } } // ═══════ AFTER: StateFlow ═══════ class UserViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow<UiState>(UiState.Loading) val uiState: StateFlow<UiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() fun loadUser(id: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = UiState.Loading try { val user = repository.getUser(id) _uiState.value = UiState.Success(user) } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = UiState.Error(e.message) } } } } // Fragment (StateFlow — lifecycle-safe collection) viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { viewLifecycleOwner.repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { viewModel.uiState.collect { state -> when (state) { is UiState.Loading -> showLoading() is UiState.Success -> showUser(state.user) is UiState.Error -> showError(state.message) } } } } // Compose (StateFlow — simplest of all) @Composable fun UserScreen(viewModel: UserViewModel) { val state by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() when (state) { is UiState.Loading -> LoadingIndicator() is UiState.Success -> UserContent((state as UiState.Success).user) is UiState.Error -> ErrorMessage((state as UiState.Error).message) } }
Key differences after migration:
1. StateFlow requires an initial value (no null initial state ambiguity)
2. StateFlow uses distinctUntilChanged by default — won't re-emit the same value
3. repeatOnLifecycle replaces observe(viewLifecycleOwner) for lifecycle safety
4. In Compose, collectAsStateWithLifecycle() is the gold standard — it stops collection when the app is backgrounded

Combining Multiple Sources — LiveData vs Flow

// LiveData: MediatorLiveData (verbose) val profile = MediatorLiveData<ProfileState>() profile.addSource(userLiveData) { user -> profile.value = ProfileState(user, postsLiveData.value) } profile.addSource(postsLiveData) { posts -> profile.value = ProfileState(userLiveData.value, posts) } // Flow: combine (clean) val profile: StateFlow<ProfileState> = combine( userFlow, postsFlow ) { user, posts -> ProfileState(user, posts) }.stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), initialState)

questions.title: RxJava Observable Types
questions.description: You need to call an API endpoint that returns a single user object. Which RxJava type is most appropriate?
options: Observable<User> — it's the most common type
Single<User> — exactly one result or an error, perfect for API calls
Maybe<User> — in case the API returns null
Flowable<User> — for backpressure on network requests
questions.explanation: Single<User> explicitly models the contract: exactly one emission (the user) or an error. Observable would work but is semantically weaker — it implies there could be multiple emissions. Maybe is for when a result is genuinely optional (like a cache lookup). Flowable's backpressure is irrelevant for a single API call.
hints: Think about the emission contract — how many items will this endpoint return?
Each RxJava type encodes the cardinality in its type signature

questions.title: subscribeOn vs observeOn
questions.description: What threads does each part of this chain run on?
questions.code:
api.getUsers()                              // Line 1
    .subscribeOn(Schedulers.io())            // Line 2
    .map { users -> users.sortedBy { it.name } }  // Line 3
    .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) // Line 4
    .filter { it.isNotEmpty() }              // Line 5
    .subscribe { users -> showList(users) }  // Line 6
options: Lines 1-3 on IO thread, Lines 5-6 on main thread. subscribeOn sets the source thread, observeOn switches downstream.
Lines 1-2 on IO, Lines 3-6 on main thread
Each line runs on its own thread
questions.explanation: subscribeOn determines where the source (upstream) executes — it affects everything above it (Line 1: the API call) and everything before the first observeOn (Line 3: the map). observeOn switches the thread for everything downstream — Lines 5 and 6 run on the main thread. A common mistake is thinking subscribeOn only affects the line it's on.
hints: subscribeOn affects the source and everything upstream
observeOn switches threads for everything downstream of it

questions.title: LiveData Memory Leak
questions.description: This Fragment leaks its observer after configuration changes. Users report duplicate toasts appearing — one more toast per screen rotation. Fix the bug.

checks.message: Use viewLifecycleOwner instead of 'this' in onViewCreated

questions.explanation: Using this (the Fragment) as the lifecycle owner means the observer is tied to the Fragment's lifecycle, not the View's lifecycle. When the Fragment is on the back stack, its View is destroyed but the Fragment survives. On return, onViewCreated is called again, adding a SECOND observer — the old one was never removed because the Fragment is still alive. Using viewLifecycleOwner ties the observer to the View's lifecycle, so it's automatically removed when the View is destroyed.
hints: What lifecycle owner should you use inside onViewCreated?
Fragment lifecycle and View lifecycle are NOT the same — the Fragment outlives its View when on the back stack

questions.title: setValue vs postValue
questions.description: What is the output when this code runs?
options: Observer receives only 3 — postValue overwrites pending values before they're dispatched
Crash: postValue cannot be called from a background thread
questions.explanation: postValue() posts a task to the main thread's message queue. If you call it multiple times before the main thread processes the first one, each call overwrites the pending value. Only the last value (3) is actually dispatched to observers. This is different from setValue(), which is synchronous and would dispatch each value immediately (but must be called on the main thread). If you need every value delivered, use setValue() on the main thread or switch to a Channel/SharedFlow.
hints: postValue queues a task on the main thread handler
What happens when multiple values are posted before the main thread processes them?

questions.title: RxJava to Flow Migration
questions.description: Migrate this RxJava search stream to Kotlin Flow. Fill in the Flow equivalents.

blanks.hint: PublishSubject maps to MutableSharedFlow, BehaviorSubject maps to MutableStateFlow
RxJava switchMap cancels the previous inner stream — which Flow operator does the same?
The Flow equivalent of subscribeOn — affects upstream execution context

questions.explanation: RxJava's switchMap (cancels previous inner observable when a new one arrives) maps to Flow's flatMapLatest. subscribeOn maps to flowOn, which changes the upstream dispatcher. The Subject maps to MutableSharedFlow (for events) or MutableStateFlow (for state). Note that flowOn only affects operators above it (upstream), just like subscribeOn.
hints: switchMap = cancel previous, use latest. What's the Flow operator with 'Latest' in its name?
flowOn changes the context for upstream operators

questions.title: LiveData Lifecycle Behavior
questions.description: A ViewModel sets a LiveData value while the Fragment is in the CREATED state (on the back stack). What happens when the user navigates back to this Fragment?
options: The value is lost — LiveData only dispatches to active observers
The Fragment receives "Hello from background!" immediately upon returning to STARTED state — LiveData replays the latest value to new observers
The Fragment crashes because its View was destroyed
The value is queued and delivered in onResume()
questions.explanation: LiveData always holds and replays the latest value. When the Fragment returns from the back stack, a new View is created, onViewCreated runs again, and the observer is registered anew. Since the LiveData already has a value ("Hello from background!"), it immediately dispatches it to the new observer once the lifecycle reaches STARTED. This is the "sticky" behavior of LiveData — it's a feature for state (you always want the latest data), but a problem for one-shot events (toasts, navigation) which led to the infamous "SingleLiveEvent" hack.
hints: LiveData stores the latest value — does it care when it was set?
What happens when a new observer is added to a LiveData that already has a value?

questions.title: LiveData to StateFlow Migration
questions.description: This LiveData-to-StateFlow migration compiles but has a subtle lifecycle bug. The UI keeps updating even when the app is in the background, wasting battery. Fix it.

checks.message: Wrap collection in repeatOnLifecycle to respect lifecycle state
Specify the minimum lifecycle state for collection

questions.explanation: Without repeatOnLifecycle, the collect call runs continuously even when the app is in the background. Unlike LiveData (which automatically pauses dispatch when the observer is not at least STARTED), Flow's collect is not lifecycle-aware by itself. repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) starts collection when the lifecycle reaches STARTED and cancels it when it drops below STARTED — restarting on return. This mirrors LiveData's built-in behavior.
hints: LiveData automatically stops dispatching in CREATED state. Flow's collect does not — what API adds this behavior?
The collection should start at STARTED and stop below STARTED

questions.title: Why Google Recommends StateFlow Over LiveData
questions.description: Name at least three reasons why Google now recommends StateFlow/Flow over LiveData for new Android projects.
questions.answer: Google recommends StateFlow over LiveData because: (1) Flow has rich operators (combine, flatMapLatest, debounce, distinctUntilChanged) while LiveData has only map, switchMap, and verbose MediatorLiveData. (2) StateFlow requires an initial value, eliminating the nullable-initial-state problem in LiveData. (3) StateFlow integrates natively with coroutines and is Kotlin Multiplatform compatible. (4) collectAsStateWithLifecycle() is the recommended Compose integration, whereas observeAsState() is considered legacy. (5) Flow supports cold streams for one-shot operations, while LiveData is always hot. (6) Testing Flows with Turbine is more expressive than testing LiveData.
questions.explanation: LiveData was designed in the Java era (2017) as a simpler alternative to RxJava. Now that Kotlin coroutines and Flow are mature, LiveData's limited operator set and Java-era design show their age. Flow and StateFlow cover LiveData's lifecycle awareness (via repeatOnLifecycle / collectAsStateWithLifecycle) while offering a far richer API. LiveData remains in maintenance mode — functional but not evolving.
hints: Think about: operators, Compose support, KMP, initial value, testing
What can Flow do that LiveData cannot?
